title: The Case of Absolute Value
tutorialText: cout << "Welcome to the tutorial! I'm The Honorable Judge Compiler, and I'll be your guide as you learn the basics of being a lawyer at the Syntax Criminal Court! Let's begin." << endl;
description: This function claims to return the non-negative absolute value of any number.

testFeedback.feedback: cout << "This test is useful because it verifies the 'positive' path of the code. It confirms that when given a normal positive number, the function returns it unchanged." << endl;
cout << "This is a high-quality test. It forces the code to enter the 'if' block, which is the only way to prove the function can correctly handle negative inputs." << endl;
cout << "This test is redundant. Since we already have a test for a positive number, adding another one doesn't reveal any new information about how the code works." << endl;

missedEvidenceExplanation: cout << "Even though your selected test cases weren't the best fit, that's okay because every case is a chance to learn. In this trial, the most important tests were at the 'boundaries.' While testing basic numbers is a good start, always look for the 'Zero' case to ensure the logic doesn't break at the edge. Below, I have explained which tests were useful, redundant, or misleading. Please review these insights before we move to the next trial." << endl;
closingStatement: cout << "The evidence shows that the function handles both positive and negative inputs correctly. Even though we didn't see every possible number, the logic holds up. I find the defendant NOT GUILTY." << endl;

tutorialText: cout << "Great job getting through your first case! Let's move on to the next one!" << endl;
title: The False Start
description: This function is supposed to greet a user by adding 'Hello ' before their name.

testFeedback.feedback: cout << "This test is a critical piece of evidence. It clearly demonstrates that the function fails to add a space, resulting in the name and greeting being smashed together." << endl;
cout << "This test is misleading. Because the input itself starts with a space (' Bob'), it makes the function look like it's working even though the code is actually broken." << endl;
cout << "This is a great edge case test. By using an empty name, we can see that the function doesn't even provide a trailing space after the word 'Hello'." << endl;
cout << "This test is redundant. We already proved the missing space error with 'Alice', so testing 'World' doesn't give the jury any new information." << endl;

missedEvidenceExplanation: cout << "Even if your evidence wasn't a perfect fit, remember that the best lawyers learn from every trial. In this case, we saw a 'misleading' test where a space in the input ( ' Bob') accidentally covered up a bug in the code. To truly catch a bug, you need 'clean' evidence that doesn't do the function's work for it. Please review the test details below to see how each piece of evidence influenced the final verdict." << endl;
closingStatement: cout << "The verdict is GUILTY. The function failed to include the required space character, relying on the user to fix the formatting themselves. The defendant is sentenced to the Debugging Ward!" << endl;

tutorialText: cout << "You're gaining confidence in the courtroom! Let's see how you handle a function with a more subtle logic gap." << endl;
descTutorialText: cout << "This function claims to handle both even and odd numbers. Review the evidence carefully to see if it actually delivers on that promise." << endl;
title: The One-Track Mind
description: I check if a number is even. I return True for evens and False for odds.

testFeedback.feedback: cout << "This test doesn't help uncover the bug. The function already handles even numbers correctly, so testing '2' doesn't reveal the missing logic for odd numbers." << endl;
cout << "This is critical evidence. It reveals that when the function receives an odd number like '3', it fails to return 'False' and instead returns 'None'." << endl;
cout << "This test also focuses on an already-working path. While correct, it doesn't help expose the missing behavior for odd numbers." << endl;
cout << "This is strong evidence. It confirms the function has no handling for odd numbers, returning 'None' instead of 'False'." << endl;

missedEvidenceExplanation: cout << "Even if your evidence wasn't a perfect fit, you're learning an important lesson about code coverage. In this case, the function was so focused on 'Even' numbers that it completely ignored the 'Odd' path. Always look for tests that check every possible outcome—if you only test for success, you'll never see where the code fails. Please review the details below." << endl;
closingStatement: cout << "The verdict is GUILTY. The defendant only did half of its job! By failing to provide a path for odd numbers, it left the program in an uncertain state. Case closed!" << endl;

tutorialText: cout << "Excellent work so far. You've moved into 'Medium' difficulty cases now. The bugs here are a bit more specific, so pay close attention to the requirements!" << endl;
descTutorialText: cout << "This function has a specific requirement for how to handle invalid inputs. Compare the expected output to the actual output very carefully." << endl;
title: The Overzealous Adder
description: I add two positive numbers. If either is 0 or less, I return 'Error'.

testFeedback.feedback: cout << "This test is misleading. While it passes, it only checks the 'happy path' and ignores how the function handles invalid inputs." << endl;
cout << "This is an essential test. It confirms that negative inputs are handled correctly by returning 'Error'." << endl;
cout << "This test is very useful. It verifies that zero is treated as invalid input and correctly returns 'Error'." << endl;
cout << "This test is redundant. We already know the function can add positive numbers; additional examples don't help validate edge cases." << endl;

missedEvidenceExplanation: cout << "In this case, there was no bug in the function itself. All inputs, including edge cases like zero and negative numbers, are handled according to the specification. Sometimes, the real challenge is verifying correctness rather than finding faults." << endl;
closingStatement: cout << "The verdict is NOT GUILTY. The function behaves exactly as specified, correctly handling both valid and invalid inputs. Case closed!" << endl;

title: The Listless Guardian
description: I return the first item in any list. If the list is empty, I return 'None'.

evidencePool.label: first([]) -> None
evidencePool.actualOutput: IndexError: list index out of range

testFeedback.feedback: Works fine when there is data, but hides the danger.
SMOKING GUN! The function crashes completely when given an empty list.
Hides the lack of a safety check.
Just more proof that it works... until it doesn't.

missedEvidenceExplanation: Accessing items[0] without checking the length of the list is a common cause of program crashes.
closingStatement: Guilty! The defendant claimed to handle empty lists but actually crashed the entire court on the first empty input!

title: The Capital Crime
description: I ensure passwords are secure. They must be 8 characters long AND contain at least one uppercase letter.

testFeedback.feedback: Proves the function ignores the 'uppercase' requirement entirely.
This password is valid, so the function appears to work.
Correctly fails for length, but doesn't test the uppercase bug.
Just another case of the length check working.

missedEvidenceExplanation: To find this bug, you must test a password that is long enough but lacks an uppercase letter.
closingStatement: Guilty! The security guard was only looking at the size of the password, letting weak, lowercase passwords right through the gate!
